content:
{
  "title": "Dynamic Unit-Test Guided Prompting for Large Language Model Code Generation",
  "problem": "Current prompting methods for code generation often fail to guide LLMs toward syntactically correct and functionally accurate code, especially for complex programming tasks. There is a need for prompting strategies that encourage models to produce code that passes task-relevant unit tests.",
  "motivation": "As LLMs are increasingly used for code synthesis, ensuring functional correctness is critical. Traditional static prompts provide insufficient feedback for nuanced requirements, while in-context examples can be brittle. Leveraging the model's ability to reason about tests during prompt construction could directly improve practical code quality.",
  "method": "We propose a novel prompting method that interleaves code generation requests with paired, minimal unit tests in the prompt. The prompt first presents a programming task, followed by one or more simple unit test functions (in pseudocode or the target language), then instructs the LLM to 'generate code that passes all the above tests.' Tests are chosen or synthesized to cover key edge cases and functional requirements. Optionally, intermediate steps ask the LLM to explain how its code will satisfy each test before producing the full implementation.",
  "experiment_plan": "We will evaluate on HumanEval, MBPP, and RepoBench datasets. Baselines include standard completion prompting and chain-of-thought prompting. Metrics will be exact match accuracy, functional correctness (unit test pass rate), and syntactic correctness. We will analyze improvements for various code complexities and ablation on test coverage in prompts."
}
